topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam on the paper, a mocking stain across the meticulously organized notes. I stared at the 'D-'. It felt like a physical blow, a sudden, sharp coldness spreading through my chest. Every word I’d poured into that essay, every late night spent wrestling with the concepts, seemed to dissolve into meaningless scribbles under the weight of that single, damning mark. I clenched my jaw so tightly my teeth ached. I didn't look up when Professor Albright finally called my name; I just kept my gaze fixed on the chipped paint of the desk, a silent, burning resentment coiling in my gut. I gathered my things with a jerky, unnatural motion, the sound of my backpack zippers sounding offensively loud in the quiet classroom.

[story 2]

"A C-minus? Seriously?" I muttered under my breath, the sound barely audible but sharp enough to cut through the ambient classroom murmur. My hands were shaking, a tremor that had nothing to do with nervousness and everything to do with pure, simmering fury. I picked up the graded paper, turning it over and over, my thumb tracing the margin where the low score resided. It wasn't just a grade; it was an indictment. A complete dismissal of everything I thought I was capable of. I shoved the paper into my bag with unnecessary force, the material protesting with a rustle. When I finally walked out, my steps were stiff, each footfall deliberate and heavy, radiating a palpable sense of being deeply wronged.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the library seemed to buzz with malicious intent, reflecting off the condensation on my water bottle. I reread the feedback, my eyes scanning the critique as if searching for some hidden, deliberate insult. The discrepancy between my effort and the result was staggering, a chasm yawning wide between my ambition and this paltry outcome. A tight knot formed beneath my sternum, constricting my breathing. I slammed the book shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the cavernous space. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping backward with a grating screech. I didn't bother to look at my classmates; they were irrelevant noise. I just needed to get away from that room, away from the evidence of this utter failure.
